actions.thought: With 9-7 offsuit, I have a very weak starting hand that lacks both high-card strength and strong connectivity for hitting the flop. In a three-handed game, while ranges can open up, 9-7 offsuit is typically a fold from the button, especially since there is no reason to inflate the pot with such a marginal holding. Conservative, disciplined play early in a 150-hand tournament is key to long-term success.

In a 3-handed game, holding QJo in the Small Blind after the Button has folded is a very strong starting hand. Since it's blind versus blind, I want to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot. A standard raise size here is 3x the big blind to 300 total chips to either win the pot immediately or play a pot with a range advantage.

We are facing a 3x open from the Small Blind. In a Blind vs Blind situation, opening ranges are extremely wide. King-Six suited is a decent hand to defend with because it has decent high-card value, flush potential, and the advantage of playing in position post-flop. Calling is the standard and most profitable play here.

I have top two pair (Queens and Jacks) on a highly coordinated board with both straight and flush possibilities. I need to bet for value and protection, charging hands like KT, T8, or diamond draws. A bet of 400 into a 600 pot is a standard size to build the pot while making it expensive for drawing hands to continue.

The flop comes 9d Qs Jd, which connects heavily with the Small Blind's preflop opening range, including many broadway cards and flush draws. We hold King-high with a mere gutshot straight draw and no backdoor flush draw. Facing a sizable continuation bet of 400 into a pot of 600, we lack both the direct equity and the implied odds to continue. Folding is the most straightforward and sensible decision.

Queen-Deuce offsuit is a very weak starting hand, even in a three-handed game from the button. It lacks both high-card strength and connectivity. While opening a wide range from the button is standard, Q2o is typically at the bottom of that range and can be easily dominated. I will fold to preserve my chip lead and wait for better opportunities.

With K8o in the small blind after the button folds, I have a hand with decent high-card value for blind vs blind play. Completing the blind (limping) is a standard strategy that keeps the pot small and allows me to see a flop cheaply. If the big blind decides to isolate with a large raise, I can comfortably fold.

I am in the big blind with a weak hand, Jack-Six offsuit. Since the small blind has completed the bet to match my big blind, the pot is 200, and there is no need to raise. Checking is the standard, low-risk play to see a flop for free.

With King high and no draws on an Ace-high flop, I have very little equity against a calling range but decent showdown value if the hand checks down. Leading out as a bluff here doesn't accomplish much, as the Big Blind's checking range pre-flop contains many Ax hands and flush draws that will continue. I will check and evaluate his action.

The board contains an Ace, which is highly likely to have connected with the opponent's range. My Jack-high has no pair or significant draw. Checking is the optimal play to maintain pot control and see the next card without investing additional chips.

With four to a straight and three spades on the board, the texture is highly coordinated. I have the 8 of spades giving me a flush draw, as well as a straight draw with any 5. However, betting into this board is risky as my opponent can easily have an Ace, a 5, or a higher spade. Checking allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity for free if it checks through, or evaluate my opponent's action if they bet.

The board is highly coordinated with possibilities for both a straight and a flush. My hand, Jack-high, has no showdown value, and bluffing into the opponent who checked twice is high-risk. Checking behind is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and see the river without further commitment.

On the river, I have King high with a missed 8-high flush draw. The pot is very small (200 chips) and a bluff has low success rate on this board given my opponent could easily have a weak pair, an Ace, or a 5. Checking allows me to get to showdown for free to occasionally beat missed draws or just concede the hand without further risk.

With a Jack-high hand and no pair on a board that contains three spades and potential straight connectors (A-2-3-4), my hand lacks showdown value. Pro has checked the river, indicating they may also have a weak hand or a bluff catcher. Betting would be a pure bluff into a potentially trapped range, which is unnecessary and risky. Checking behind allows me to reach showdown with zero additional risk.

Queen-Nine offsuit is a marginal hand, even when acting first from the button in a 3-handed game. It's easily dominated by broader calling ranges (like AQ, KQ, QJ, QT) and doesn't play particularly well post-flop. To minimize risk early in the tournament and avoid playing weak hands, folding is the safest and most solid play.

Ten-Seven offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position from the small blind. I prefer to fold and preserve my stack early in the tournament rather than committing more chips with a marginal holding.

I am on the button with 3h 7s, which is an extremely weak hand with no playability. Even in a 3-handed game, this hand does not justify an open or a call. Folding is the standard and correct strategic decision to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

In a 3-handed game, when the Button folds, the Small Blind should play a wide range against the Big Blind. King-Seven offsuit (K7o) is well above the median hand and is strong enough to open for a raise. Raising to 300 (3x) puts pressure on the Big Blind and allows me to take control of the hand with the initiative, aiming to win the pot immediately or play post-flop with a range advantage.

Opponent raised to 3x from the Small Blind. I am holding Q8o in the Big Blind. Although I would have position post-flop, Q8o is a weak offsuit hand that is often dominated by the Small Blind's raising range. With 100 BB effective stacks, there's no need to defend such a marginal holding. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve chips.

I am on the button with King-Four suited in a three-handed game. This is a standard hand to open with for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and utilize my positional advantage. K4s has good playability and equity against the blinds' defending ranges. I will maintain my previous opening size of 300 (3x the big blind) to remain consistent and build a pot while in position.

I have Pocket Queens (Qc Qs) in the Small Blind. Flash opened from the Button to 3bb. Pocket Queens is a premium hand, significantly ahead of a standard Button opening range. 3-betting is mandatory for value, to build the pot, to maintain the initiative, and to discourage the Big Blind from calling and making it a multi-way pot. A raise to around 10bb (1000 chips) is a standard out-of-position 3-bet size.

I am in the big blind with 7d 9d. The small blind has 3-bet to 1000 after the button raised to 300. This is a strong 3-bet, and I am out of position against two players. My suited connector is not strong enough to call a significant 3-bet OOP, as it is difficult to realize equity and perform post-flop. The pot odds do not justify a call, and I should preserve my stack for a more advantageous spot.

Pro has 3-bet from the Small Blind after my button open. K4s is a hand that plays reasonably well in position, and the pot odds (3:1) are too good to fold, especially with the potential to outplay the opponent post-flop. I'll call the 3-bet and see a flop.

The flop is quite dry (6h Tc 5d) and my Pocket Queens are an overpair, giving me a very strong hand. Flash's pre-flop flatting range on the button against a 3-bet usually consists of medium pocket pairs (77-JJ), suited connectors, and broadways like AK, AQ, or AT. I want to extract value from worse hands like JJ, AT, 99, 88, and 77, while denying free cards to overcards that have 6 outs against me. A smaller bet size around 1/3 of the pot (700 chips) is optimal on this dry texture to keep his floats and marginal made hands in the pot while safely building it.

Pro 3-bet from the Small Blind pre-flop and is now leading with a continuation bet of 700 into a 2,100 pot on a 10-6-5 rainbow board. My King-high with a backdoor flush draw is extremely weak against a 3-betting range that likely includes overpairs (JJ-AA), strong Tens (ATs, KTo), and high-equity overcards. While the pot odds are 4:1, I have very little equity to continue and no reliable way to win the hand without an expensive bluff. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.
Holding A6o on the Button 3-handed is a standard open. I'll min-raise to 200 to steal the blinds with a decent high card, while minimizing risk if I face a 3-bet.

I am in the Small Blind with a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand (3-4 offsuit). Playing this hand out of position against a raise from the button is negative expected value. Given the early stage of the tournament, I should conserve my stack and wait for a more playable hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs 5d. The Button (Pro) has raised to 200. With 100 already posted, I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, getting 3.5:1 odds. Although Q5o is a weak hand and I will be out of position, the price is good enough to defend against a standard Button opening range in a 3-handed game. I will call to see the flop and evaluate.

I have flopped top pair with a weak kicker (Queens, 5) on a very dry board (Q-7-4 rainbow). Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Pro), the standard play is to check to him. This allows him to continue with his wide button-opening range, including bluffs or weaker hands. I don't want to lead into him and fold out his bluffs, nor do I want to bloat the pot yet with a marginal kicker. I will check and plan to call a standard continuation bet.

On a dry Q-high rainbow flop, as the pre-flop raiser I have a significant range advantage. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot should be enough to fold out my opponent's air and win the pot immediately with Ace-high.

I have flopped top pair with a 5 kicker on a very dry board. The opponent's bet of 150 into 450 is a standard continuation bet. With top pair, I have a clear call here. Raising is unnecessary as it would likely fold out his bluffs and weaker hands while potentially isolating myself against better Queens or sets. By calling, I keep the pot size controlled and allow the opponent to continue bluffing or value betting with a wider range on the turn.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a board that hasn't changed much on the turn, except for adding a possible diamond draw and some straight potential. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to control the pot size and encourages the opponent to continue bluffing with their air or betting with marginal hands that I still beat. I don't want to build a large pot with a mediocre kicker, so I will check and see how the opponent responds.

The turn gives me bottom pair with my six, while I still retain an Ace kicker. By checking behind, I can control the size of the pot and realize my hand's showdown value. Betting here would likely only fold out weaker hands and get called by better hands like Qx, 7x, or mid-to-high pocket pairs.

On the river, the 5c appears to have improved my hand to Two Pair (Queens and Fives). However, the board is now quite coordinated (4-5-6-7), meaning any 8 or 3 in the opponent's hand completes a straight. Since I was the one who checked the turn and the opponent (Pro) checked behind, it's possible they are also playing a marginal hand cautiously or were waiting for a draw. While my Two Pair is strong, the straight potential makes leading out risky. By checking, I can control the pot size and decide whether to call a bet based on the sizing, or simply take the hand to showdown if the opponent checks back. Given the action so far, checking is the most prudent way to realize my equity without over-committing to a potentially beaten hand.

On the river, the board brings a 5, putting a four-card straight (4-5-6-7) out there. I only have a pair of sixes with an Ace kicker, which is essentially just bluff-catching value at this point. Betting here serves no purpose because better hands (like any 8, any 3, a Queen, or a Seven) will call or raise, and worse hands (like busted draws) will simply fold. Checking behind allows me to realize my showdown value and keeps the pot controlled.

6h7s is a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability and low equity. As the button in an unraised pot, there is no need to commit chips to a marginal hand. The standard, disciplined play is to fold and conserve my stack for stronger cards and more favorable positions.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Jack of clubs, a premium hand in a 3-handed game, especially when the action is folded to me. I want to raise for value and to seize the initiative against the Big Blind. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately.
K6o is a weak hand to defend against a 3x raise from the small blind, even with position post-flop. The odds and playability do not justify a call.

I have suited semi-connectors on the button in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening hand to apply pressure to the blinds. I've used a 3x opening size consistently in previous hands, so I'll stick with 300 to maintain a balanced range and leverage my positional advantage.
T3s is a very weak hand to play from the small blind against a button raise, especially with the big blind still to act behind me. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Holding 2-6 offsuit is a bottom-tier hand with no playable equity. Being out of position against an aggressive button raiser, I have no incentive to defend my big blind with such a weak range. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound choice to preserve my stack for a better spot.

A6 suited is a strong hand to open with on the Button in a 3-handed game. I'll make a standard 2.5x raise to try and steal the blinds or play a pot in position with a hand that has good flop playability and nut flush potential.

Holding K7s in the small blind against a button open is a standard calling hand in a 3-handed game. The button's range is wide, and my hand has decent post-flop potential. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, the pot odds justify a call to see the flop.

I have King-Five offsuit in the Big Blind. Pro raised to 250 from the Button, and Flashlight called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 600, and it costs me only 150 to call. This gives me 4-to-1 pot odds. While K5o is not a particularly strong hand to play out of position, the excellent price and the fact that I am closing the action make this a standard defend. I will call and see a flop.

I am in the small blind with middle pair (sevens) on a board of Qc-8s-7h. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Pro) and also have the Big Blind (Flash) behind me, leading out with a bet is weak as I will rarely get called by worse and might be raised off my equity. Checking is the standard play to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on the aggression of the pre-flop raiser.

I have K5o on a board of Qc-8s-7h. I have no pair and no direct draws. In a multi-way pot against the pre-flop raiser (Pro) and the Small Blind, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. I have no reason to lead here, as I'm unlikely to fold out better hands or get called by worse. I will check and see how the Button responds.

With Ace-high and backdoor clubs in a 3-way pot, betting on a board that connects reasonably well with both blinds' defending ranges (8x, 7x, 98, T9, Qx) is risky. Checking back allows me to take a free card to potentially hit an Ace or pick up the nut flush draw on the turn.

I am in the small blind holding a pair of sevens with a King kicker. The board is Q-8-7-9, which is quite coordinated and potentially connects well with the opponents' ranges (many straight draws and pairs possible). Since everyone checked the flop, the pot is relatively small (750). Checking again is the most prudent play here to maintain pot control and see if the others are interested in the pot. If someone bets, I can easily fold my weak pair, and if everyone checks, I get a free card to potentially improve on the river.

The turn card is the 9h, which creates a very connected board with several straight possibilities (J-10, 10-6, 6-5). I have King-high with no draw and zero showdown value. Since the Small Blind (Flashlight) has checked to me, my best course of action is to check as well. I have no reason to bluff into two opponents on such a coordinated board, especially when I have no equity. I will check and hope the Button (Pro) checks back so I can see a free river card.

With a highly connected board of Q-8-7-9, the ranges of the blinds connect very well here. Any opponent with a Ten or Jack has a strong draw or a made straight. While my 6 gives me an open-ended straight draw, hitting a Ten could give someone else a higher straight, and a 5 isn't the nut straight either. Taking a free card is the most prudent play, as a bluff into two opponents on this wet texture is unlikely to get folds from pairs or strong draws.

The board is highly coordinated (Q-8-7-9-A) with a potential flush completion on the river. My pair of sevens is a weak holding, essentially a bluff-catcher, but with the check-check-check-check action, it is unlikely anyone has a monster, though the Ace is a concerning card. I have no incentive to bet, as I would only be called by better hands, and checking allows me to potentially see the showdown without risking more chips.

The board is highly coordinated (Qc-8s-7h-9h-Ah) and I have King-high with no showdown value. Both the Small Blind (Flashlight) and the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) have checked throughout the hand. While the Ace on the river is a scare card that I could potentially represent with a lead, bluffing into two opponents on such a connected board is risky. Since Flashlight has checked to me, I will also check and see if the Button (Pro) checks back to go to showdown. I expect to lose this hand most of the time, but checking minimizes my losses.

I rivered top pair with my Ace, but the board is extremely wet, featuring three hearts and a highly connected texture (7-8-9-Q). Both opponents checked to me, so my hand has decent showdown value, but a value bet here is incredibly thin. If I bet, weaker pairs like Queens or Eights will likely fold, and I'll mostly get called by better hands such as flushes, straights, two pairs, or Aces with a better kicker. Checking back secures a free showdown and is the safest play.

5d 3c is a very weak hand with poor potential for post-flop equity. Playing this hand would likely lead to unfavorable post-flop situations without any significant strength to back it up. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to preserve my stack.

In a blind-vs-blind situation, Ace-Jack offsuit is a very strong hand. I will raise to 300 to take initiative and build the pot against the Big Blind, despite being out of position for the remainder of the hand.

JTs is a very strong suited connector that plays excellently in position. Since this is a blind-vs-blind situation and Flash raised to 3x from the Small Blind (which is typically a wide range), calling is a standard and highly profitable play to see a flop in position.

I have a range advantage on this King-Queen-high board as the pre-flop raiser. Although I currently only have Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw, a continuation bet is appropriate here to pressure the Big Blind's marginal holdings and maintain the initiative on a board that generally favors my opening range.

We flopped an open-ended straight draw (any Ace or 9 gives us the nut straight) along with a backdoor flush draw. Facing a half-pot continuation bet of 300 from the small blind, we are getting 3:1 pot odds, which makes calling very profitable. Calling allows us to realize our strong equity in position without risking being blown off our draw by a re-raise, keeping the pot manageable.

After my flop continuation bet was called, the 3d on the turn doesn't change the board texture significantly. Pro's range for calling the flop contains many Qx and Kx hands that are unlikely to fold to a second barrel. I will check to control the pot size and see if I can realize my equity (gutshot and Ace outs) for free or a small price on the river.

We still have our open-ended straight draw, but no flush draw since the turn is a diamond. Flash's check suggests weakness or pot control. By checking behind, we take a free card to try and hit our straight on the river without risking a check-raise that would force us off our draw. If we hit our straight, we can bet for value; if we miss, we can evaluate a bluff on the river.

On the river, the board is 4c-Qd-Kh-3d-9h. I have Ace-high (Ah Jd). After my flop continuation bet was called and the turn went check-check, it's unlikely that Ace-high is the best hand if we go to showdown, as Pro's flop-calling range is condensed around pairs (Queens, Kings, or middle pairs). However, Pro's turn check suggests they also have marginal showdown value and might not be willing to call a large bet. That said, trying to bluff Pro off a Queen or a King on this dry board is risky. Checking is the most prudent move; I will hope to win against missed draws or potentially fold if Pro bets, as Ace-high is a weak candidate for a hero call in this spot.

The river 9 completes our open-ended straight draw, giving us a King-high straight. Flash checking the turn and river suggests he has a marginal made hand like a King or a Queen, or a missed draw. Since we have a very strong hand (only losing to exactly A-T), this is a clear spot to bet for value. A bet of 800 (about two-thirds of the pot) is sized to extract maximum value from bluff-catchers that will call hoping we missed a draw.

On the river, the board is 4c-Qd-Kh-3d-9h. I have Ace-high (Ah Jd). After my flop continuation bet was called and the turn went check-check, Pro's range is likely condensed around marginal showdown value hands like a Queen or a King. Pro's bet of 800 into a 1200 pot (2/3 pot) on the river is polarizing. Given that Pro checked the turn, they might be betting a King for value or executing a bluff with a missed draw like J10. However, Ace-high is at the very bottom of my range that reached this point, and I lose to all of Pro's value range (any pair). Based on Pro's previous play (Hand #6), where they checked down top pair, this bet likely represents either a strong King or a polarized bluff. Without a better read that Pro is over-bluffing this specific line, calling 800 with Ace-high is too risky. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Qs6d. While it's a 3-handed game where opening ranges are wider, I have been raising frequently in previous hands (Hands 1, 4, 5, 7, 8, and 10). Continuing to raise with marginal hands like Q6o might invite a 3-bet or a light call from Pro or Flashlight. Given my current image and the weakness of the hand, folding is the most disciplined play to maintain a solid image.

K2 suited is a reasonable hand to play blind versus blind. Against an opponent who has shown a tendency to fold relatively often, an open raise from the small blind puts pressure on their wide big blind range. Raising to 300 chips attempts to steal the dead money immediately, while still giving us a suited King to fall back on if called.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d 7d. The Small Blind raised to 300, and the Button folded. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win 400), 37 suited is a weak hand to defend out of position against a 3x open. Playing such a low-equity hand OOP will likely lead to difficult decisions on later streets, and folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

Ten-Eight offsuit is a relatively weak starting hand. Even on the button, it is usually best to fold this rather than attempt a steal against the blinds, as it lacks good equity and playability post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with T5 suited. Completing the blind is a standard heads-up play against the Big Blind, as the cost (50 chips) is low relative to the potential of seeing a flop with a suited connector. The pot odds justify the call to see if I can hit the board.

I have Ad 9c in the Big Blind. The Button has folded, and the Small Blind has limped in. A9o is a very strong hand in this heads-up scenario. I will raise to 400 to take control of the pot and extract value from the Small Blind's wide limping range.

Ten-five suited is a weak starting hand to play out of position against a raise. Given that Flash has shown aggression in previous hands and I am at a positional disadvantage, calling a raise of 300 chips is negative expected value. Preserving my stack for better opportunities is the disciplined approach.

I am dealt 5-10 offsuit on the button. This is a weak starting hand with very poor playability, and calling the big blind of 100 to see a flop is negative expectation. Given the current tournament status and the competitiveness of my opponents, it is better to conserve my stack and wait for a more premium hand to engage with.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 4h, which is a very weak starting hand. Flashlight has already folded on the button. Playing 84 offsuit out of position against Pro in the Big Blind is generally a losing play. While I could attempt a steal, the hand has zero equity if called and very little post-flop playability. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Jack-Trey offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand. While I have the opportunity to steal the blinds, J3o is generally not strong enough to open-raise with, especially since Pro (SB) has shown willingness to call or defend. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

With Q7 offsuit in the small blind and the button folded, I'm faced with a marginal situation. Q7o is too weak to play profitably out of position against the big blind. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve my chip lead without getting involved in a tricky post-flop scenario with a weak hand.

I have 99 on the Button, which is a strong pocket pair in a 3-handed game. I want to raise to build the pot for value, gain control of the hand, and thin the field. A standard 2.5x open to 250 is appropriate here.

I am in the small blind with 6s 2s, which is an extremely weak hand. Facing a raise from the button (Pro), calling 200 chips to play out of position against both the button and the big blind is strategically unsound. The risk-to-reward ratio is poor, and folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Four offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Although the price to call is relatively small (150 more to win a total pot of 400), Q4o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Pro has shown aggression in previous hands, and I don't want to commit chips with a hand that is easily dominated and has little post-flop potential. Standard play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the button position with 9c-5d, which is a very weak starting hand with little potential. Given the strength of this hand, it is a clear fold pre-flop. There is no strategic reason to invest chips into the pot with this holding.

I have been dealt pocket fives in the Small Blind. After the button folds, I have a strong enough hand to raise for value and to protect my position. A standard 3x raise to 300 chips is consistent with my previous aggressive pre-flop play and aims to either take down the blinds or play a heads-up pot with the initiative.
With 45o, our hand is too weak to defend against a 3bb raise, even in a blind vs blind situation. Folding is the most profitable play here to preserve our stack for better opportunities.

I am on the Button with Qc 7h, a marginal but playable hand in a 3-handed game. My strategy so far has involved frequent 3x raises from late position, which has been successful in picking up blinds as both Pro and Flashlight have folded often to pre-flop aggression. I will continue this pattern by raising to 300, aiming to take down the pot immediately or play the hand with positional advantage.

A9o is a marginal offsuit ace. Facing a 3bb button open, playing it out of position from the small blind is a tricky spot. Calling is unappealing as it invites the big blind to squeeze or overcall, and if we hit an ace, we might still be outkicked by hands like AT+. 3-betting is an option, but A9o works better as a fold in a standard strategy against a 3x open. Folding is the safest and most solid decision here.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 7d, facing a raise from the Button to 300. The pot is offering decent odds, and suited connectors play well in a defending range against a button open. Calling is the standard and most profitable play to see a flop and potentially realize equity with my draw-heavy hand.

I am the out-of-position caller on a dry board (K-5-5) that favors the pre-flop raiser. With nothing but 8-high, I have no reason to lead. Checking is the standard play, allowing me to evaluate the opponent's continuation bet or see if they check back.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with positional advantage on a very dry, paired board (Kd-5c-5h). Flashlight checked to me, which often indicates they have missed this flop. By placing a continuation bet of roughly half the pot, I am representing a King or a high pocket pair, both of which are strongly in my button opening range. This bet should be sufficient to fold out most of Flashlight's hands that do not contain a King or a Five.

The board texture of K-5-5 is extremely dry and favors the pre-flop raiser, who likely has a King, a pocket pair, or at the very least a range advantage. My hand, 8d 7d, has no equity, no pair, and no draws. Calling a continuation bet of 300 into a 950 pot with 8-high on this specific board is mathematically incorrect and lacks strategic merit, as I have almost no path to winning the hand by showdown or forcing a fold.

King-Eight offsuit is a relatively weak starting hand, even on the button. Since the Big Blind has been active and aggressive, attempting a steal with a marginal holding like K8o may lead to tricky post-flop spots or facing a 3-bet. Folding is the most straightforward and solid play here to conserve chips.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit after the button (Pro) has folded. This is a heads-up situation against the Big Blind (Flash). Ace-Eight is a strong enough hand to play aggressively in this blind-vs-blind scenario. A raise is preferred over limping to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand going into the flop.

K5s is a strong hand to defend the Big Blind against a raise from the Small Blind. Given that I will have position post-flop and only need to call 200 more into a 400 pot, I have more than enough equity and potential to justify seeing a flop.

The flop of Ks, Qs, 9d is very wet and hits the Big Blind's defending range quite hard, as they likely hold many Kings, Queens, and mid-range connectors. With only Ace-high and no draw, I have very little equity or showdown value here. Betting into a board that is so well-connected for the opponent would be a low-percentage bluff that is likely to be called by a significant portion of their range. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and evaluate the turn card, as I have limited potential to improve my hand.

On this King-high board, I have flopped top pair with a King. My kicker (5) is weak, but Flashlight has checked to me after being the pre-flop raiser. This check could indicate a trap or a weak hand that missed this coordinated board. I will bet 300 to take the initiative and protect my hand against various draws (straight and flush) that are present on this board.

I hold the nut flush draw with the Ace of Spades on a board of Ks-Qs-9d. While the board is heavy and hits the opponent's range, having the nut flush draw provides significant equity, especially with the Ace of Spades being the highest possible card of the suit. Calling the 300 bet to see the turn card is the mathematically sound decision, as I have roughly 3-to-1 pot odds (300 to win 900) which justifies continuing with the draw.

I am holding the Nut Flush Draw with the Ace of Spades on a board of Ks-Qs-9d-2c. Since I am out of position and holding a draw rather than a made hand, checking is the optimal strategic play. Checking allows me to control the pot size and evaluate the opponent's action. If Flash bets, I have the pot odds and equity to continue with my draw. If Flash checks behind, I effectively gain a free card to try and hit my flush on the river.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (5) on a very coordinated board. Flashlight called my flop bet and has now checked twice. While I might be ahead of draws or a weaker pair, my kicker makes me vulnerable to any better King. By checking back, I control the pot size and can evaluate the river based on the final board texture and Flashlight's action. This minimizes risk on a board that contains many potential straight and flush draws.

I have paired my eight on the river, giving me a pair of Eights. While this is a marginal hand, it does have some showdown value. My opponent checked the turn, suggesting they might also have a weak hand or a missed draw. Betting into them would likely only be called by better hands, so checking is the best way to control the pot size and hopefully reach a showdown where my pair of Eights is good.

On this river, the 8d doesn't change the board texture significantly. Flashlight has checked for the third time after calling my flop bet. My top pair of Kings with a 5 kicker is likely the best hand against a range that includes missed draws (spades, JT) or a Queen. However, any better King or a slow-played straight (JT) beats me. Since there is little value in betting against a range that likely only calls with better hands or splits, I will check back to realize my showdown value and minimize risk.

Pocket fours are a playable hand in position, and opening with a raise is the standard strategic move to take initiative and build a pot. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a balanced size that discourages callers while maintaining control of the action.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 2s, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight opened from the button with a raise to 300. While I have a suited hand, its high-card value is non-existent and I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position. Folding is the most prudent move to preserve my stack for more favorable situations.

Q8o is a relatively weak offsuit hand that does not play well out of position. Facing a 3x open from the Button, the most mathematically sound decision is to fold, as defending with this hand often leads to marginal, dominated post-flop spots.

I'm on the Button with Queen-Ten offsuit in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening hand. My strategy so far has involved consistent 3x raises when entering the pot. I'll maintain that sizing here to exert pressure and potentially take down the blinds or see a flop with the advantage of position.

I am in the Small Blind with an unsuited, disconnected garbage hand (6h 9s). The Button (Flash) has opened for 3 BBs. Folding is the only sensible play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Q5 suited is a reasonable hand to defend the big blind with against a button raise. Given the pot odds and the stack-to-blind ratio, calling is the standard play here to see a flop with a decent range, rather than folding or over-committing with a raise.

I am in the Big Blind and it is my turn to act on the flop. The board of A-6-6 with two diamonds gives me a flush draw with my Queen-high flush draw (Qd-5d). Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flash), checking is the standard play to control the pot size and see the turn card. Betting into the aggressor here is risky as my hand is strictly a draw.

I hold a Queen-high flush draw on an Ace-high board with paired sixes. Flash has placed a standard c-bet into the pot. With favorable pot odds and significant equity from my flush draw, calling is the optimal play to see the turn card. Folding would be too conservative, and raising would be ill-advised given that I am currently behind any Ace or six, and my opponent's range likely contains high cards or small pocket pairs that are currently beating me.

I am in the Big Blind (out of position) and it is my turn to act on the turn. My hand is a Queen-high flush draw on a board of A-6-6-K. Since I am out of position, checking is the optimal play to maintain pot control and see the river card for free, as my hand is currently a draw and betting would likely be a risky semi-bluff against a range that could easily contain an Ace or King.

On the turn, the board is Ad 6d 6s Kh. Flashlight checked to me for the second time after calling my flop continuation bet. My hand, Qc Th, has no current value and very little equity to improve. While the King is a decent scare card, Flashlight's call on the flop (Ad 6d 6s) indicates they likely have a piece of the board, such as an Ace, a Six, or a flush draw. Betting again here is risky because an Ace is unlikely to fold, and I would be bloating the pot with total air. Checking back allows me to see a free river, control the pot size, and potentially represent a stronger hand later or simply give up if the river doesn't improve my situation or provide a better bluffing opportunity.

The river card failed to complete my flush draw, leaving me with Queen-high, which is effectively a bluff catcher or a surrender hand on this A-K-8-6-6 board. Since my opponent checked back the turn, it is possible they have some showdown value or are also playing a hand that missed. Betting would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from any Ace, King, or even an Eight. Checking is the most prudent action to reach the showdown with the current pot size and avoid unnecessary risk.

On the river, the board is Ad 6d 6s Kh 8s and Flashlight has checked for a third time. My hand, Queen-high (Qc Th), has no showdown value against any pair or King-high. However, my check on the turn after a flop continuation bet was a pot-controlling move. While Flashlight's check on the river could signal weakness, they called a bet on a paired Ace-high board, which strongly suggests they have at least a piece of it (a weak Ace, a small pocket pair, or a Six). Given the board texture and my previous check, a bluff here is unlikely to force a fold from an Ace or even a medium pocket pair. I will check back and concede the pot if beaten, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

83 offsuit is an extremely weak and unplayable hand. Despite having position on the button, there is no value in attempting to steal the blinds or play this hand, especially with a deep stack. Folding is the only reasonable choice here.

With pocket sixes in the small blind and the button having folded, I have a strong hand heads-up. Raising is the best play to take initiative, define the opponent's range, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or build value with a strong holding.

In a blind-vs-blind situation, [Qd, 9c] is a strong enough hand to defend against a raise from the Small Blind, especially since I will have position post-flop. The pot odds of 2:1 (200 to call a pot of 400) make this a profitable call against a typical wide SB opening range.

The flop is 5d Kh 5h, which is a wet board that connects well with a Big Blind's calling range (lots of Kings, suited connectors, and possibly a five). The board is 5d-Kh-5h, which is quite dry and generally favors the pre-flop raiser's range. However, Flashlight (SB) has checked. Betting here might be met with a check-raise or a call from a sticky pocket pair, and I'm not yet ready to turn my hand into a pure bluff.

The board texture is static on the turn with a Jack added, and my pocket sixes act as a marginal bluff-catcher. Betting does not offer significant value as I would likely only be called by better hands, such as Kings, Jacks, or a five. Checking is the standard play to control the pot size, keep the betting sequence neutral, and see if I can get to a showdown cheaply.

On a board of 5d-Kh-5h-Js, the action has been checked through both the flop and the turn. The board has run out 5d-Kh-5h-Js-3d. With my pocket sixes, I have a marginal showdown hand. Flashlight (SB) raised pre-flop but has since checked three consecutive streets (Flop, Turn, and River). Queen-deuce offsuit is a very weak starting hand with poor playability. Even in a 3-handed game, playing this hand from the button is negative expected value. It is best to fold and conserve my stack for a more favorable hand.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit in the small blind, which is a very strong hand three-handed. Flashlight has folded from the button. The action is now on me in the small blind. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing for this position.

K3o is a weak unsuited hand with a poor kicker. Even though we are facing a blind-versus-blind steal attempt and will have position post-flop, this hand lacks the equity and playability to profitably defend against a raise. Folding is the disciplined and standard choice.
I am on the button with Pocket Tens (TT), which is a premier starting hand in a three-handed game. I will make a standard 3x open-raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand.

I am in the small blind with 8d 2h, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a pre-flop raise to 300 from the Button, there is absolutely no reason to play this hand. The mathematically and strategically optimal decision is to fold.

I hold pocket Kings, a premium hand, in the big blind against an aggressive button open from Flash. To maximize value and take control of the hand, I will 3-bet. A raise to 950 is an appropriate sizing to build the pot and potentially induce a call or a 4-bet, given my opponent's wide opening range.

I started the action with an open-raise to 300 holding Pocket Tens, a top-tier hand in a 3-handed game. Flashlight has responded with a 3-bet to 950 from the Big Blind. Calling allows me to use my positional advantage post-flop and keeps Flashlight's range wide, including hands I dominate or bluffs that might fold to a 4-bet. I hold pocket Kings on a dry board with [7d, 3s, 7s]. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have the range advantage. I hold an overpair (TT) on a relatively safe, paired board (7-7-3). Flashlight 3-bet pre-flop and is now leading out with a continuation bet of nearly half the pot. Raising here might isolate me against better hands like JJ-AA, while folding would be far too tight given the board texture.

The board has become significantly more dangerous with the four of spades on the turn, completing a potential flush draw and pairing the board, which could also benefit a hand with a seven. My pocket Kings have been devalued on this board, as I am likely behind any made flush or a seven. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or hands that are already crushing me, while forcing weaker hands to fold. Therefore, checking is the most prudent action to control the pot size and get to a showdown as cheaply as possible.

On the turn, the four of spades completes a potential flush. I hold the Ten of spades, giving me a flush draw to go along with my overpair (TT). Flashlight, who was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, has now checked. This check could represent a variety of hands: a made flush or trips checking to trap, a hand like AA/KK with a spade looking to check-call, or a hand like AK/AQ that has given up. Given the board texture and my position, checking back is the most prudent play. It allows me to control the pot size, see a free river card to potentially improve to a flush, and keeps the pot manageable if I am currently behind a flush or a set. Betting here likely only gets called by better hands or draws that have significant equity against me.

The board shows 7d-3s-7s-4s-8h, which is quite wet with flush possibilities and potential straight draws. I hold pocket Kings, which are essentially a bluff-catcher on this board. Since the turn was checked through, I have no reason to bet the river; if I bet, I will likely only be called by hands that beat me, and if I am betting for value, there are very few hands worse than mine that would pay off a bet given the board texture. Checking is the most prudent action to reach showdown as cheaply as possible and minimize potential losses against a better hand.

I hold Pocket Tens (TT) on a board of 7-3-7-4-8 with three spades. I have the Ten of spades (Ts), which is a significant blocker. Flashlight 3-bet pre-flop and led the flop but has now checked both the turn and the river. While I might get value from a smaller pair or a stubborn Ace-high, there's a risk of being check-raised by a trap (like a slow-played flush or trips). Since I have solid showdown value and the pot is already sizable, checking back is the most prudent play to guarantee winning against the majority of Flashlight's range while avoiding a potentially disastrous blow-up against a monster hand.

76o is a weak offsuit connector. While we are on the button and could attempt a steal, this hand lacks the equity and playability needed to be a profitable open, especially when facing opponents who can 3-bet or defend. The best move is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ten-Deuce offsuit. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has just called the completion, and the Button (Pro) has folded. Betting here with middle pair out of position is risky, as it might force out hands I'm beating while inviting callers with better hands or stronger draws. I have an open-ended straight draw with the Ten of Hearts on an Eight-Nine-Jack board. I am holding a pair of nines on a board of J-J-9-8. While I have showdown value, my hand is vulnerable to any Jack or a better pair. The pot is very small (200), and there has been no aggression from my opponent so far. Checking allows me to see the river for free and avoid inflating the pot with a mediocre hand that is likely beaten if my opponent shows any resistance.

On a board of 8-J-9-J, I hold an open-ended straight draw (7 or Q). The Small Blind has checked to me twice in this limped pot. While betting could potentially take the pot down now, checking back allows me to see the river for free and realize my equity without the risk of being check-raised off my draw. With 10-high, I have no showdown value, so I am hoping to hit my straight on the river or possibly bluff if the river is a safe card and they check again.

With the river card bringing the third Jack to the board, my hand (holding a Nine) has improved to a full house, Jacks full of Nines. I should bet for value to extract chips from any hand that might call, such as a lower pair or an eight.

On a board of 8-J-9-J-J, the community cards provide Three of a Kind (Jacks). My hand, Ten-Deuce, gives me a final hand of J-J-J-T-9. I am on the button with an Ace-high hand in a three-handed game, which is a strong enough position and hand strength to open the pot. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard aggressive move to take the initiative, put pressure on the blinds, and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Four offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand that does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. I am on the Button with Ten-Trey offsuit. This hand is extremely weak and does not warrant an opening raise, even in a three-handed game. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for better opportunities.

With Q6o in the small blind and the button having folded, this is a fairly weak, disconnected offsuit hand to play out of position against the big blind. While a steal attempt could sometimes work, I have no significant card advantage and it's safer to just fold and wait for better spots.

Q6 suited is a weak marginal hand. Although I am on the button, there is no need to try and steal the blinds with such a hand early in the tournament with a healthy stack of over 11000 chips. I will fold and wait for better opportunities.

On a monotone board of Ad 7d Td, my hand (5h 3h) has zero equity. Flashlight (the pre-flop aggressor) checked. I am in the small blind with King-high and no pair, flush draw, or open-ended straight draw. The board is extremely wet with three diamonds and potential straight combinations. I am in the small blind with King-high on a board of Ace-Ten-Seven-Queen-Seven. My hand has no showdown value and essentially acts as a bluff catcher that loses to almost any pair or Ace. I have 5-high with absolutely no showdown value on a board of Ad-7d-Td-Qc-7c. I am holding 8-2 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand with no connectivity or strength. Calling from the small blind out of position against a button raise with such a poor hand is negative expected value. It is best to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable hand.

6-8 offsuit is a weak, non-connected hand that lacks showdown value and is unsuitable for playing from the button. There is no strategic reason to invest chips into the pot with this holding, so folding is the disciplined and correct action.

On a board of Ad-Qd-7h-9c, I have bottom pair (sevens). The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) checked the flop, which could indicate weakness or a trap, but in a three-way pot, it's more likely they missed or have a marginal hand. Flashlight (SB) has checked both the flop and the turn. Given my weak pair and the presence of two overcards (Ace and Queen) that hit the opponents' ranges well, I should check to keep the pot small and attempt to reach a cheap showdown. There is little value in betting as I am unlikely to fold out better hands or get called by worse.

With two overcards and a 9 on the board, my pocket sixes have very limited equity. Both opponents have checked again, indicating weakness or draws, but trying to bluff two players off hands like a 7, 9, or weak Queen is unnecessary and risky. Checking allows me to control the pot size and hopefully reach a cheap showdown.

I have a pair of Queens (Qs on a board of Ad Qd 7h 9c 3h). Both opponents have checked through the flop and turn, which indicates they likely do not have a strong Ace or a set. However, a pair of Queens is extremely vulnerable, and any Ace in their range beats me. Betting is unlikely to generate value as they would only call with better hands and fold worse ones. Checking is the most prudent action to reach showdown cheaply.

On the river board of Ad-Qd-7h-9c-3h, I hold bottom pair with my seven. The action has been checked all the way through on the flop and turn by all three players. Flashlight (SB) has checked again on the river. My hand has some showdown value in a pot where no one has shown any aggression since pre-flop. Betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands, and checking allows me to potentially see a cheap showdown. If I check and Pro checks behind, I may win against missed draws or lower pocket pairs. Therefore, checking is the most prudent action.

The river brings a brick with the 3 of hearts. Both opponents have checked three streets in a row, indicating they likely have marginal hands or missed draws (such as diamond flush draws, JT, or KT). My pocket sixes have decent showdown value against these missed draws. Betting here would be a thin value bet at best and a bluff at worst; it's unlikely to get called by worse hands like 55 or 44, and I don't want to get called by a trapped Ace, Queen, or a paired 7 or 9. Checking behind is the safest and most logical play to realize my showdown equity without risking more chips.

I am in the button position with 8-2 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand with very little playability. There is no strategic advantage to calling or raising with this holding, so folding pre-flop is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Deuce offsuit. While King-high has some potential against the Big Blind, I am out of position and my stack is significantly smaller than the Big Blind's (Pro). I am on the button with 5h 6c, which is a very weak offsuit hand. I am in the Small Blind with an off-suit, gapped 8-high (6h 8c). The Button folded, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind. This hand is too weak to profitably raise as a steal or limp in out of position. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve chips.

I am on the Button with a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand (6c Th). With two players left to act, including the big blind who has a short stack and might shove over a steal attempt, folding is the standard and safest play.

I am in the small blind with 8h 4s, which is a very weak hand. Since the button (Pro) has folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. Given the poor quality of my hand and the risk of the big blind raising, folding is the most disciplined and prudent play to preserve my stack.

My hand, six-ten offsuit, is objectively weak and lacks the equity required to justify an open-raise from the button. While I have the largest stack at the table and could theoretically afford to steal, this specific hand does not provide a strong enough foundation for a bluff, so folding is the most disciplined and optimal play.

I am in the Small Blind with a medium pocket pair (7h 7c). Since Flashlight on the button has folded, it is down to a blind versus blind battle. Pocket sevens are a very strong hand in this situation. I will raise to 300, which has been my standard opening size, to take control of the pot and potentially win it immediately if the Big Blind folds.

Blind vs blind, Flash raises 3x from the small blind. I hold A4o. Ace-high is decent here, and I have the advantage of playing in position post-flop. A call is standard to evaluate the flop, whereas 3-betting against his relatively short stack might force us into a high-variance spot if he shoves.

I have a pocket pair of sevens on a board with two overcards (Queen and Nine). Since I was the pre-flop aggressor in a blind versus blind situation, a continuation bet is standard here. A bet of 300 (half the pot) allows me to take down the pot immediately if the opponent has missed, and protects my hand against hands like overcards that might have equity. If the Big Blind calls or raises, I will have to evaluate carefully as my hand is vulnerable to many turn cards.

The flop is Q-9-2 rainbow. I only have Ace-high with a weak kicker and no draws. Flash c-bets half the pot. Given his relatively small remaining stack size, trying to bluff or float with A4o is unprofitable and too risky. Folding is the standard and safest option.

I am on the button with 7-5 offsuit. While I have been opening frequently to maintain aggression, this hand is too weak to justify a raise even in a three-handed game. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my 30bb stack for better opportunities.
K3o is a weak offsuit hand. Being out of position against the chip leader in the Big Blind makes it an easy fold. There's no need to force a steal or play a weak hand post-flop.

I am on the Button with 7s 6s, a suited connector that plays very well in position. Opening with a raise allows me to potentially steal the blinds or take control of the hand post-flop if called. I'll raise to 2.5x the big blind.
Ace-Seven suited is a solid hand to play from the small blind. The pot odds are favorable to call and see a flop against the Button's opening raise, especially with the potential for a flush.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 7c. Pro raised to 250 from the Button, and Flashlight called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 600, and it costs me only 150 more to call. Given the pot odds and the deep stacks of my opponents, calling here is a standard play to see if I can connect with the board.

I have top pair with an Ace, but a weak 7 kicker. In a 3-way pot, betting out into the pre-flop raiser and the big blind could be risky as I may be outkicked. Checking allows me to evaluate the actions of my opponents and control the pot size while holding a marginal top pair.

I have 7-high on an Ace-King high flop in a three-way pot. The board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Pro). With no pair and no real draw, I have no reason to bet. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet.

With a 7-high flush draw on an Ace-King high board against two opponents, checking behind is a solid play. While a continuation bet could represent an Ace or King, the board heavily interacts with the calling ranges of the blinds (Ax, Kx, broadways). Checking ensures I see a free turn card to realize my flush equity without risking a check-raise that would force me to fold my draw, especially given Flash's shorter stack.

I hold a pair of Aces with a weak 7 kicker on a coordinated board (Ac Ks 4s Ts) that presents multiple threats, including a potential spade flush and straight draws. Given the 3-way pot and the fact that the action was checked around on the flop, it is prudent to continue checking to control the pot size. Betting out here could lead to being outkicked or facing a raise from an opponent holding a better Ace or a made flush, so keeping the pot manageable is the most strategic approach.

I have 7-high with no draw on a board that is extremely coordinated (Ace, King, Ten, and three spades). Since I am in a three-way pot and have absolutely no equity or showdown value, there is no reason to bluff here into two opponents who might be trapping or holding a piece of this board. I have turned a made flush with my 7s6s on a board with three spades. Since opponents typically hold at most one spade, my hand is almost certainly best. I must bet to protect my hand by charging these draws, while also extracting value from one-pair hands like an Ace or King. A bet of 500 gives opponents incorrect pot odds to chase their one-card flush draws.

The board has become very dangerous with three spades (Ks 4s Ts) and potential straight possibilities. My Ace-high pair (with a 7 kicker) is a marginal hand that is likely beaten by any made flush, a better Ace, or two-pair combinations. Given that the Pro is betting into two other players on a highly coordinated turn, it is safer to fold rather than chase a marginal hand that lacks significant equity, especially as I do not hold a spade to protect against flush draws.

I have 7-high with no pair and no draws on a very dangerous board containing an Ace, King, and three spades. Pro, the pre-flop raiser, has bet 500 into the pot after the turn. Given that I have zero equity and no showdown value, and considering that Flashlight has already folded, there is no reason to continue in this hand. Calling or raising with 7-high here would be a waste of chips.

Holding 6-5 offsuit is fundamentally weak with poor playability, especially in a three-handed game. I am currently the chip leader and have no incentive to contest pots with speculative, low-value cards. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in hold'em. With the action folded to me in the Small Blind, King-Ten offsuit is a strong hand to open with. Raising to 3x the big blind puts pressure on the Big Blind, often taking down the pot immediately, while still retaining good post-flop playability if called.

Qc 2s is a weak hand, particularly out of position against a raise. It does not play well post-flop, and defending the big blind with such a bottom-tier holding against a 3x raise is negative expectation. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here.

Holding 98o on the Button is a marginal hand at best. With Flash sitting on a shorter stack of about 26 BBs in the Big Blind, attempting a steal here is risky as we could frequently face a 3-bet jam. Folding is the most prudent and standard play with this offsuit connector.
Stealing the blinds from the small blind. K6o is a reasonable hand to attack the big blind's range, and applying pressure to the shorter-stacked big blind is a solid strategic move.

I am in the big blind with 9d 5d. Flashlight, who is the chip leader, has raised to 300 from the small blind. The pot odds are 2:1 to call (200 to win a total pot of 600). A-J offsuit is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game, especially on the button. I have position on both opponents, so I should raise to build the pot and take the initiative, putting pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with KQs, a very strong starting hand. Flashlight, the chip leader, has raised to 300 from the Button. My hand is definitely strong enough to call or raise. Holding an offsuit one-gapper (7s5c) in the Big Blind facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, the best move is to fold. The hand plays poorly out of position and has very low equity against multiple opponents.

On this flop of Ts Jh 6h, I have two overcards and an open-ended straight draw (any 9 or Ace gives me a straight). Since I currently have no made hand but significant equity with the straight draw and overcards, I will check and see how they respond. If they bet, I have sufficient equity to call and potentially realize my draw on the turn.

I have top pair (Jacks) on a flop of Ten-Jack-Six. As the pre-flop aggressor, I should continue betting to extract value from weaker hands and put pressure on any potential straight or flush draws. A bet of 350 into the 700 pot is a standard continuation bet size to maintain control and build the pot.

On a flop of Ts Jh 6h, I have an open-ended straight draw (any 9 or Ace completes the straight) and two overcards (King and Queen). Flashlight has bet 350 into a pot of 700, giving me 3:1 pot odds. I need approximately 25% equity to make this call profitable. With 8 clean outs for a straight, I have roughly a 17% chance to hit on the turn and a 32% chance by the river. When adding the potential value of my overcards and the backdoor flush draw with the King of hearts, my total equity easily exceeds the required 25%. Given Flashlight's aggression and my stack size, calling is preferable to raising, as it allows me to realize my equity and potentially trap if I hit my draw on the turn.

On the turn, the board pairs with a 6s. I still hold an open-ended straight draw (any Ace or Nine) and overcards. Since I am out of position and the board has paired, which might favor my opponent's range or simply change the board texture, I will check again. This allows me to see if Flashlight will check behind, giving me a free card to hit my straight, or if they will bet, allowing me to evaluate the pot odds for a call with my remaining stack.

I have top two pair (Jacks and Sixes) on a board of T-J-6-6. The turn 6s is a safe card, though it completes any spade draws. I should bet for value against any remaining Tens or smaller pairs and to charge any drawing hands that might be chasing a flush or straight. A bet of 700 (half pot) is appropriate to build value while still keeping Flash in the hand if they are holding a weaker pair or a draw.

I currently have an open-ended straight draw (any Ace or 9) and two overcards (King and Queen). With 8 clean outs for the straight, I have approximately a 17.4% chance to hit on the river. The pot is currently 2100, and it costs me 700 to call, giving me pot odds of 3:1. This requires roughly 25% equity to be a profitable call. When factoring in the possibility that my overcards are live against a portion of Flashlight's range (like a lower pair or a heart draw) and the potential to win more if I hit my straight on the river, calling is the most statistically sound decision. Folding would be too tight given the equity, and raising into the chip leader's aggression with just a draw is unnecessarily risky for my tournament life.

The river Queen of Spades gives me top pair with a King kicker. While I missed my open-ended straight draw, I now have significant showdown value. However, the board is dangerous as any Ace-King or King-Nine has a straight, and any Six has trips. Given Flashlight's relentless aggression on previous streets, I will check to control the pot and potentially induce a bluff from missed heart draws or straight draws like 98. Betting into the chip leader here likely only gets called by hands that beat me.

The board is T-J-6-6-Q. My hand is A-J (Top Pair). Flash has check-called two streets, which suggests a Ten, a lower pair, or a draw that may have missed. The river Queen makes a range of hands like Q-X better than my Jacks. Betting for value is thin, as worse hands like Tens might fold or might not call, and better hands like Queens (which may have been drawing or floating) have me beaten. Checking back allows me to take the showdown without risking more chips against a potential trap or a rivered Queen, while still capturing the pot against weaker hands like small pocket pairs or missed draws.

I am on the button with 3s Jc, which is a weak hand. Given J3o is a very weak hand and I am against two players with significant stacks (Pro has 10.5k, Flashlight has 15k), there is no reason to play this hand from the button. I will fold.

With QJo in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a strong enough hand to attack the Big Blind. Raising to 300 is my standard opening size; it puts pressure on Flashlight to defend with marginal hands and allows me to take the betting initiative into the flop.

I am in the big blind with 4-5 offsuit, a very weak hand. Even though the pot odds are tempting and it is only 200 more to call, playing this hand out of position against a raise from the small blind is not a profitable strategy long-term. With a solid stack, I can afford to wait for better cards.

I am on the Button with a very weak, unsuited hand (T4o). It has virtually no playability post-flop and doesn't block enough strong hands to consider a steal, especially with the Big Blind having around 40 big blinds. An easy fold.

I am in the big blind with 5s2c, a very weak hand. Flashlight, who is in the small blind, has raised to 300. Given the strength of my hand and my position, there is no reason to defend or bluff here. Folding is the standard play with such a low-equity hand against a raise.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 5h. Flashlight, who has a significant chip lead and has been playing aggressively, raised to 300 from the Button. I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Six offsuit, which is a very weak, disconnected hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, it is an easy fold, especially since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

I am on the button with AKo, the best non-paired hand in poker. This is a clear raising hand to build value and isolate. I've been relatively active with pre-flop raises of 300, and sticking to this consistent sizing is effective. I will raise to 300.

With King-Queen offsuit in the Small Blind facing a Button open, we have a strong hand that dominates many of the Button's opening ranges. If called, we have a hand that plays reasonably well post-flop, though we will be out of position.

A6 suited is a marginal hand out of position. Facing an open from the button and a subsequent 3-bet from the small blind, my hand is likely crushed by the ranges of my opponents. Continuing in this bloated pot out of position would be highly unprofitable. Folding is the disciplined, professional choice.

I hold Ace-King offsuit on the button, which is a premium hand. I initially raised to 300, and Pro (the Small Blind) has 3-bet to 900. Facing a 4-bet from the Button, King-Queen offsuit is severely outclassed. Calling out of position against this tight range, especially with such a low stack-to-pot ratio remaining, is a losing play. The most optimal decision is to fold and preserve our stack.
I am on the Button with Ten-Seven offsuit (Td 7c), which is a very weak starting hand. There's no reason to get involved in this pot, so folding is the optimal and standard play.

I hold pocket Jacks in the small blind with the button having folded, leaving me heads-up against the short-stacked big blind. This is a very strong hand in this spot, so I will raise to build the pot and put pressure on the big blind. A raise to 300 is an appropriate sizing to take control of the hand.

I am in the big blind with King-Nine offsuit. Flashlight, who is the chip leader, has raised to 300 from the small blind. However, calling 200 more into a 400 pot gives me good odds (2:1), and I have position on the small blind post-flop. I have flopped a set of Jacks on a board of Qc Ac Jc. I am holding King-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten) on an extremely coordinated monotone board (Qc-Ac-Jc). While I have the 9 of clubs, it is a very weak flush draw on this board. Ace-Eight offsuit is a playable hand from the button position. It has enough strength to justify an opening raise to steal the blinds or take initiative in the pot. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing that balances value with a reasonable risk profile.

I am in the Small Blind holding Queen-Deuce suited. Flashlight opened for 3x from the Button. While the hand has some potential in a multi-way pot due to being suited, calling here puts me out of position for the rest of the hand against the chip leader. Q2s is significantly below the standard defending range in the Small Blind against a 3x open. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better spots.

Queen-Four offsuit is a weak hand. Facing a 3x open raise from the button, there is no value in defending such a marginal, disconnected holding out of position. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am on the button with Jc6s. The current pot is 150 and it's 100 for me to call. With action folded to me in the small blind, I have Queen-Seven suited, which is a very reasonable hand to attempt a steal with or play heads-up. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the big blind and allows me to take down the pot uncontested or play a decent suited hand post-flop with the betting lead.

Q3o is a very weak starting hand, especially against a raise from the small blind. The hand has poor equity and limited post-flop potential. With a large stack, there is no need to defend this weak range, so folding is the disciplined choice.
I have 76o on the button, which is too weak to play, even in a 3-handed game. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips for better starting hands.

With the button having folded, I am in a heads-up situation against the big blind. Qc7s is a reasonable starting hand for a blind-versus-blind scenario, and I have a significant stack advantage. Raising to 300 allows me to apply pressure and potentially take down the pot pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ac 6h. The Button (Pro) has folded, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300. I have the advantage of acting last on all subsequent betting rounds post-flop. Ac 6h is well ahead of a typical wide opening range from the Small Blind in a three-handed game. My hand (Qc7s) has completely missed, offering no pair or significant draw potential. I hit middle pair (sixes) with an Ace kicker on a low, connected board. Since Flashlight checked to me, I have the opportunity to take the lead. My hand is currently very strong in a blind versus blind scenario, but the board is coordinated with straight and flush possibilities. The flop is extremely coordinated (3-5-6), which heavily favors the Big Blind's calling range. I am on the button with 4s and 2c, which is a very weak starting hand with little to no playability. There is no reason to invest any chips, so folding is the only reasonable decision.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit after the Button has folded. This is a very strong hand for a blind-vs-blind battle. Looking at previous hands, the Big Blind (Pro) has a high frequency of folding to Small Blind opens (Hands #68, #62, #53, #47). I will put in a standard raise to 300 to capitalize on this tendency and take down the blinds or play a post-flop pot with a range advantage.

Facing a raise from the small blind in a blind vs. blind scenario, King-Jack offsuit is a strong hand. Calling allows me to keep the opponent's wider opening range intact and lets me play the rest of the hand with a positional advantage post-flop. A 3-bet might isolate me against better hands, so flatting is the optimal play here.

I have flopped top two pair (Queens and Tens) on a board that is relatively dry but could contain some straight draws for my opponent (like KJ or J9). I want to bet for value against weaker hands like a single Queen, a Ten, or draws. A standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot) is appropriate to build the pot while my hand is likely the best.

We flopped an open-ended straight draw with King-Jack on a Queen-Ten-Six board. Facing a half-pot continuation bet, we are getting decent odds to call and try to hit our straight (any Ace or Nine gives us the nuts). Since we are in position, flatting allows us to realize our equity without risking a 3-bet shove from the relatively short-stacked opponent that would force us off our draw. We can re-evaluate on the turn depending on the card and our opponent's action.

I currently hold top two pair (Queens and Tens) on a board of Qh-6s-Td-7h. While the Seven of Hearts completes a potential straight for a hand like 89, I am still ahead of the vast majority of my opponent's calling range, which includes hands like QJ, Q9, JT, and various straight draws like KJ or J9. Without sufficient direct pot odds and limited implied odds due to the opponent's remaining stack size, continuing to chase the draw is mathematically unprofitable.
