I am in the big blind with Ace-Six offsuit. The button has opened and the small blind has called. I have excellent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 900) and closing the action pre-flop allows me to see a flop with an Ace, which is generally strong enough to defend here given the price.

We flopped the absolute nuts with the Ace-high flush on a heavily coordinated and scary board (4h 3h 5h). If we lead out directly, we risk folding out any hands that don't contain a high heart, a set, or a strong straight draw. By checking to the pre-flop raiser (Flash), we give him the opportunity to continuation bet with a much wider range, including overpairs or bluffs. We can then evaluate whether to smooth call to keep Flashlight in the pot or check-raise to build the pot immediately.

The flop is extremely wet with three hearts, giving several potential flush and straight combinations. My hand, Ace-high with no heart, is very vulnerable. Since the small blind has checked, I should also check to proceed cautiously and control the pot, as I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (the button) and have no made hand to warrant a bet.

I have flopped a set of fours on a highly coordinated and monotone board (4h 3h 5h). While a set is a very strong hand, it is vulnerable to any completed flushes or straights, and many turn cards could be problematic. Both opponents checked to me, the pre-flop aggressor. I need to bet here for value against hands like one-heart draws (especially the Ace or King of hearts) and straight draws, while also protecting my hand. Checking back would give a free card to draws that could easily overtake me. A bet of roughly half the pot should suffice to charge the draws without overcommitting if someone has already flopped a flush.

We flopped the nut flush on a very wet board. Flash's continuation bet of 500 into a 900 pot shows strength, likely indicating a smaller flush, a straight, a set, or a strong draw with the King or Queen of hearts. By check-raising now, we build the pot immediately, extract value from his strong made hands or draws, and protect against a 4th heart hitting the board, which could kill the action if he doesn't hold a high heart. Raising to 1500 puts immense pressure on his remaining stack and sets us up perfectly to get all his chips in the middle.

The board is extremely wet with three hearts, which completes potential flushes. My hand, containing the six of diamonds, gives me the current straight (3-4-5-6). Pro's raise to 1500 is aggressive, but with a made straight, I have too much equity to fold, despite the threat of a flush. I need to call to see the turn, as I do not have a heart in my hand to contest a flush if the board doesn't pair, but I have a strong enough hand to continue.

I have flopped a set of fours, which is an extremely strong hand, but the board is very dangerous (monotone hearts and straight possibilities). After my continuation bet of 500, Pro check-raised to 1500 and Flashlight cold-called. This sequence usually indicates very strong hands, such as a made flush or straight. However, my remaining stack is only 2175. If I call the 1000, I will have only 1175 left in a pot that will be over 5000, making me effectively pot-committed. Even against a made flush, a set has approximately 34% equity to improve to a full house or quads by the river. Given the pot odds and my relatively short stack, folding a set is not an option. Moving all-in is the best play to realize my equity and maximize my potential return if I improve or if I am currently ahead of semi-bluffs or smaller sets.

With the nut flush on the flop, I am currently crushing both opponents. Flash's re-raise to 2500 and Flashlight's cold-call of my initial 1500 raise indicate immense strength from both players, likely representing smaller flushes, sets, or flopped straights. I want to play for stacks right now to extract maximum value and protect my hand against board-pairing turn cards that could give an opponent a full house, or a 4th heart that could kill the action. Shoving all-in capitalizes on their perceived strength and commits the rest of my chips while I am a massive favorite.

The board is monotone with three hearts (3h-4h-5h), which presents a massive risk as any single heart gives an opponent a flush. I hold the six of diamonds, giving me the current straight, but I have no heart in my hand to defend against a flush. There is heavy aggression with a raise to 8550 in front of me, which strongly suggests that at least one opponent has a made flush or a very strong hand like a set that is likely to improve or already ahead of me. Committing the majority of my remaining stack here without a flush is mathematically and strategically unwise. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I have flopped a set of fours on a 5h-4h-3h monotone board. While Pro's massive check-raise and subsequent four-bet shove strongly indicate a made flush, I am holding an extremely strong hand with approximately 24% equity to improve to a full house by the river. Given the current pot of 13,450 and my remaining stack of only 975, I am getting pot odds of nearly 14-to-1. Mathematically, I only need about 7% equity to make this call profitable. Therefore, calling to realize my equity is the only correct play.

We are now playing heads-up, and I am on the button (Small Blind) with Ace-Five suited. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play with excellent playability post-flop, equity, and the ability to make nut flushes or straights. I should definitely open with a raise to take the initiative, build the pot, and apply pressure. A standard 2.5x open to 250 chips is optimal here.

K-2 offsuit is an extremely weak hand to defend with from the big blind. Although the pot odds are decent to call, playing a weak hand out of position against an aggressive opponent like Pro is a losing proposition in the long run. I will fold and conserve my stack for a better spot.

With [Kc, 7c] in a heads-up situation from the small blind/button, I have a decent starting hand that holds its own in positional play. Raising to 300 allows me to apply pressure to the big blind, take control of the initiative, and potentially win the pot immediately or set up a favorable post-flop scenario.
Q2o is a very weak hand to play, especially out of position against a pre-flop raise. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

We are heads-up and I am on the Button (Small Blind). King-Nine offsuit is a perfectly fine hand to open-raise with to apply pressure and try to steal the big blind or play a heads-up pot in position. I'll open to 2.5x the big blind.

Holding Jack-Deuce offsuit is a very weak hand. Even though the pot odds are attractive to defend the big blind, I have minimal equity against a standard raising range from the button, and playing this hand out of position will be difficult. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am in the small blind with a marginal suited hand, Jc7c. Heads-up, this is a playable hand to see a flop, but not strong enough to warrant a raise. Calling the additional 50 chips to complete the big blind is the standard approach to see the flop cheaply.

I have T7o in the Big Blind and the Small Blind (Flashlight) just limped. T7o is a weak offsuit hand, so raising to steal or build a pot here is unnecessary and risky out of position. Checking gives me a free look at the flop to try and hit a favorable board.

I have bottom pair (7) and a ten kicker on a monotone flop with three hearts (Ah 8h 7h). I do not hold a heart in my hand. This board is extremely dangerous as my opponent can easily hold a flush, a strong draw, or a better pair. My hand has very little equity to improve. Checking is the safest and only logical play, and I will likely fold to any aggression.

I have a pair of 7s on a dangerous board featuring an Ace and three hearts. My hand is currently weak, as any Ace beats me, and the board is extremely coordinated for potential flush draws. Betting here serves little purpose as I will likely fold to a raise or get called by better hands. Checking behind is the most prudent action to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have two pair, Aces and Sevens, but my Sevens are the bottom pair on a very coordinated board with three hearts. The opponent checked the flop, which could mean they are slow-playing a flush, holding a marginal hand like an 8, or completely missed. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands and risks getting raised by better ones. Checking to see a free river or evaluate the opponent's action is the safest and most logical play.

I have a pair of 7s on a board with two Aces and a potential flush draw. My hand has very limited showdown value, and it is likely that the opponent either has an Ace or is looking for a cheap river card. Betting here is unlikely to force a fold from a better hand and will likely only isolate me against stronger holdings. Checking back is the most prudent approach to keep the pot small and see the river for free.

The river brings a fourth heart to the board. I only have two pair (Aces and Sevens) with no heart, meaning any heart in my opponent's hand beats me with a flush, and any Ace or Eight also beats me. There is no reason to bet here as worse hands will fold and better hands will call or raise. Checking is the only sensible option.

The board shows four hearts and two Aces, making it a very dangerous board. My hand (pair of 7s) has very low showdown value here. Any heart makes a flush, and any Ace makes trips or better. Since my opponent checked, they might be trapping or have a weak hand that beats mine. Betting serves no value as I will only be called by better hands and fold out the hands I currently beat. Checking behind is the safest and most logical play to go to showdown.

With Ace-Deuce suited on the Button heads-up, this is a very strong starting hand that plays well post-flop. I want to open with a raise to take the initiative, potentially win the blinds right away, or build a pot with a hand that has excellent equity and flush potential.

The opponent (Pro) has raised to 2.5 big blinds from the button, which is a standard opening size. Holding Queen-Eight offsuit in the Big Blind, the pot odds dictate a call (150 to win a total pot of 400). While Q-8 is not a premium hand, it is well within my calling range for the Big Blind defense against a button open, allowing me to see a flop cheaply.

The flop is Ace-high with two clubs. My hand (Queen-Eight) has missed the board entirely, offering no pair and no strong draw. As the pre-flop caller in the big blind, I am out of position. The flop is Ace-high with two clubs, which is a board that hits the pre-flop aggressor's (Pro) range very hard. My hand, Queen-Eight offsuit, has completely missed and offers no pair, no flush draw, and minimal backdoor potential. Calling a continuation bet out of position with pure air against a likely strong range is not a profitable long-term strategy. Since I have no meaningful equity to continue, the disciplined play is to fold and wait for a more favorable spot.

Jh 5c is a weak hand, but heads-up from the small blind, defending with a call is standard when the cost is just 50 chips to see a flop, especially since I have already posted 50. Folding is too tight, and raising with this hand is unnecessary. I will call to see the flop.

With 6d 8c offsuit, I have a very weak hand. Since the opponent just limped in from the Small Blind, I can check and see a free flop. There is no reason to bloat the pot out of position with this holding.
With 8-high and no meaningful draws on a dry K-high board, my hand has virtually no equity. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop limper and evaluate. If they bet, I will easily fold.

The board is 4d 3s Kc and I hold Jh 5c. I have missed the flop entirely and have no pair or viable draw. With 8-high and no draws on the turn, my hand has virtually zero showdown value. Since the pot is small and there's no strong reason to attempt a bluff when the opponent could still be trapping or holding a marginal made hand, checking is the safest and most standard play.

With 8-high on a river that completes a four-card straight and possible flushes, I have zero showdown value. I have rivered a pair of fives (Jh 5c with the board 4d 3s Kc 3d 5d). The opponent has checked throughout the entire hand, which implies they have either a marginal hand or complete air. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands, as any king, pair of fours, or pocket pair would beat me or call me, and checking allows me to realize my showdown value against any total bluff. I have completely missed the flop with Queen-high and have no meaningful draws. Given that I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the most prudent action is to check and re-evaluate based on the opponent's reaction.

I am in the big blind with Queen high on a board of Tc-9c-6h-2h. I have no pair and no real draw. Since the opponent (Pro) checked both the flop and the turn, they are signaling weakness. I have Queen-high on a board of Tc-9c-6h-2h with no pair, no draw, and no showdown value. The opponent checked the flop but has now bet 300 into a 500 pot on the turn. I hold a premium hand (Ace-King offsuit) in a heads-up scenario. This is a clear raising hand to build the pot and exert pressure on the opponent. A raise to 300 is standard given the blind structure and my previous aggressive play.

I am dealt Jack-Two offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a 3x raise from the button while out of position in the big blind, playing this hand would be highly unprofitable. Folding is the standard and mathematically correct choice.

I am dealt Pocket Tens (Th Td) on the Button in a heads-up match, which is a very strong premium hand. I want to raise for value and to take the initiative pre-flop. A standard open to 2.5x (250 chips) is a solid sizing to build the pot while not overcommitting if faced with a massive 3-bet.
Defending my big blind with 6-7 offsuit against a button raise. Getting 2.33:1 pot odds makes this a mathematically sound call, even though I will be playing out of position post-flop.

I have an open-ended straight draw with 6s-7c on a 5d-Jh-8h board. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard play to see how they respond, while keeping the pot manageable or potentially setting up a check-raise as a semi-bluff.

With Pocket Tens on a Jack-high board, I have second pair. I have an open-ended straight draw with 6s-7c on a 5d-Jh-8h board. The pot is 750 and it costs me 250 to call, offering 3:1 pot odds. This is a mathematically sound call, especially with implied odds should I hit my straight on the turn.

I am in the big blind with an open-ended straight draw (6-7 on a 5-J-8-Q board). As I am out of position, checking is the standard and most prudent play to control the pot size and see the river card. This allows me to realize my equity if the opponent also checks, or to evaluate the pot odds and implied odds if the opponent decides to bet.

The Queen on the turn is a bad card for my Pocket Tens, as it completes straight draws like T9 and connects with overcards that might have floated the flop (like AQ, KQ). There's no need to turn my hand into a bluff right now, as it still retains showdown value against missed flush and straight draws. I have completely missed my straight draw and hold 7-high, which has zero showdown value. The opponent checked back the turn, which narrows their range but implies they likely have some marginal showdown value or are pot controlling. Betting here would be a pure bluff into a capped range, but since I have no equity and limited blockers, checking to showdown is the most standard and disciplined play.

The river brings the 3 of diamonds, a complete brick that changes nothing about the board texture. Flashlight checks again, suggesting they likely have a marginal made hand or a busted draw (like a missed heart flush or missed straight). Betting here with third pair (Tens) is too thin for value, as better hands like a Queen or Jack will call, and worse hands will simply fold. My hand retains solid showdown value against their missed draws, so checking behind is the optimal play to realize that equity safely without risking more chips.

Holding 5-3 suited in the small blind heads-up, completing the small blind is a standard strategic move. It allows me to see a flop cheaply with a hand that has good connectivity and playability, especially given the stack depth.

AdQd is a premium starting hand, especially heads-up. After completing the small blind, the opponent in the big blind raised to 350. Calling 250 more to win a total pot of 700 gives me 2.8:1 pot odds. Holding 5h-3h, a suited connector, provides enough equity and playability to warrant a call in this spot, especially considering the position.

With top pair, second-best kicker on a somewhat draw-heavy board (two hearts), a continuation bet is mandatory. I want to extract value from weaker aces (A8-AJ), middle pairs, and heart flush draws. Betting half-pot (350) accomplishes this while giving draws poor odds to continue.

I have bottom pair (treys) and a nut flush draw on a rainbow board with an Ace. The pot odds are 3:1 (350 to win 1050), and my equity with the flush draw alone makes this an easy call. Raising as a semi-bluff is an option, but with a pair of treys and a decent stack size, taking the free card or realizing equity on the turn is a more controlled and disciplined approach.

The 2d turn improves my hand significantly, giving me the nut diamond flush draw to go with my top pair, good kicker. I want to continue betting to extract value from weaker aces (like A8-AJ), charge heart flush draws, and build the pot in case I hit my flush. Betting around 70% of the pot (1000) puts maximum pressure on weaker hands and drawing hands while ensuring we get value.

The opponent has led out for 1000 into a 1300 pot. With a nut flush draw (holding the 5h), a pair of treys, and a gutshot straight draw, I have significant equity to improve on the river. My pot odds are approximately 2.3:1, requiring about 30% equity to make a profitable call, which I comfortably have given my 12+ outs. The river brings the 4h, completing both a potential flush (three hearts on board) and a four-card straight (A-2-3-4). Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands and opens me up to being raised. I have flopped and turned a strong flush draw, and the river (4h) has completed my flush with the 5h in my hand. My best five-card hand is an Ace-high flush (Ah, 7h, 5h, 4h, 3h). Given the opponent checked on the river, this is a clear value bet opportunity. I will bet 1500 to extract value from any pair or straight the opponent may have, as my hand is extremely strong and it is unlikely they have a higher flush given they checked.

The river brings the 4h, putting three hearts and a four-card straight (A-2-3-4) on the board. My pair of Aces is now essentially a pure bluff-catcher. Without a heart in my hand to block flushes, I am beaten by almost their entire value range, and there aren't enough logical bluffs to justify calling. Folding is the safest and most optimal play.

With 54s on the button, we have a nice suited connector that plays well in position. An open raise to 2.5x (250) applies pressure to the big blind and allows us to take control of the hand, masking our actual holding while having good post-flop playability.

I am in the big blind with 9c 4d, a very weak hand with poor playability. Holding Queen-Jack offsuit in the small blind during a heads-up match, I have a strong enough hand to take the initiative. Raising to 300 is a standard move to build the pot and pressure the big blind, allowing me to potentially take down the hand pre-flop or have a positional advantage post-flop.

A5o is a solid starting hand in heads-up play, definitely strong enough to defend from the big blind against a standard button raise. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without bloat, maintaining a manageable pot size out of position.

With an A-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3) on a dry, disconnected flop, checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard and safest play. It allows me to evaluate Flashlight's action and decide if I should continue based on the size of a potential c-bet.

I am the pre-flop aggressor holding overcards (Queen-Jack) on a dry, uncoordinated board (Ten-Four-Deuce rainbow). The opponent checked the flop, which often indicates they missed or are playing passively. A continuation bet of 300 (50% of the pot) is a standard strategic move to maintain initiative, define the opponent's range, and potentially win the pot immediately if the opponent has nothing.

The opponent makes a half-pot continuation bet on a dry, disconnected flop. With Ace-high, a gutshot straight draw to the wheel, and an overcard, my hand has decent equity against a wide c-betting range. Getting 3:1 on a call, peeling one street is standard to realize this equity while keeping the pot manageable out of position.

The turn card does not improve my hand, leaving me with Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw. This allows me to control the pot size and evaluate my opponent's turn action before committing more chips.

Checking behind allows me to take a free card to potentially hit a Queen or Jack on the river, or simply to realize my equity and keep the pot size manageable without committing more chips to a potentially losing hand.

On the river, I hit a pair of fives with an Ace kicker. Since the opponent checked back the turn, their range is likely capped at marginal pairs or Ace-high. Betting here wouldn't get called by many worse hands and opens me up to being raised or called by a better pair. Checking allows me to realize my showdown value or potentially catch a bluff from missed overcards or draws.

I am at showdown with Queen-Jack high on a board of T-4-2-8-5. Betting now would be a pure bluff into a potentially sticky opponent who has checked to me. Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity. From the small blind, it's a straightforward fold to avoid playing a marginal hand out of position post-flop, even if heads-up.

With 9-8 offsuit in the small blind, the pot odds are favorable to call the 50 chip difference to see a flop. My opponent limped in from the Small Blind. I am in the Big Blind with 5c 2h, an extremely weak hand. Since it costs nothing to see the flop, I will check and take a free flop rather than folding or bloating the pot with a bluff.

Betting would likely be a bluff against a range that could be trapping or simply checking a weak pair or draw. On a scary four-to-a-broadway board (A-K-J-T) where any Queen makes a straight and any pair higher than a 5 beats my bottom pair, my hand has virtually no showdown value. The board (K-A-T-J-5) is highly coordinated and strongly hits the calling ranges. Holding 9-8 high, I have zero showdown value. We are heads-up on the button with 98o. This is a standard and playable offsuit connector in a heads-up dynamic. Raising to 2.5x (250) allows us to take the initiative, potentially steal the big blind, and play in position post-flop if called.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong hand in heads-up play, especially from the Button/SB position. I want to take the initiative, build the pot while I have the lead, and leverage my positional advantage to pressure the Big Blind. A raise to 300 is a standard, effective size here.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 3bb raise from the Button. My hand is 3d2c, which is the absolute bottom of my range and the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. It has no high card strength, no suitedness, and very poor straight potential. The only logical play here is to fold.
